main_summary.summary: Scott Bates, professor of French at the University of the South in Sewanee, Tennessee, recounts his trajectory from Chicago through World War II to his lifetime in Sewanee and decades of civil-rights work in Tennessee. Born 1923 in Evanston, Illinois, Bates was drafted in 1942, attended the Army's ASTP French program at the University of Chicago, landed at Utah Beach on D-Day, and supervised military-government brothels at Avranches as a French interpreter. After completing his PhD at the University of Wisconsin, he took a one-year teaching post at Sewanee in 1954 that became a lifetime appointment. Bates joined Highlander Folk School's board of directors in 1958, remaining on it through the organization's relocation. He worked with Septima Poinsette Clark, Myles Horton, Esau Jenkins, and Guy Carawan, helped found the Sewanee NAACP chapter under Clark's supervision with Mrs. Fowler as local leader, and was present at the Highlander workshop where Horton told the SNCC students they had to do it themselves. Bates fought to desegregate the University of the South and to admit women, organized anti-Vietnam War activity through the Fellowship of Reconciliation and War Resisters League, and compiled anti-war poetry that War Resisters League published as a 13,000-copy collection.
key_themes: Highlander Folk School and movement halfway houses
Desegregation of Episcopal seminaries and Sewanee
World War II military government in Normandy
NAACP chapter organizing in rural Tennessee
main_summary.historical_significance:
This interview is a primary-source account from a white faculty insider on Highlander Folk School's board of directors during the years that bridged its labor-organizing roots and its civil-rights-movement role. Bates places himself at the 1957 Highlander workshop attended by Martin Luther King Jr. and Rosa Parks (the photograph later used in segregationist 'Communist Training School' billboards), and at the workshop that preceded the Raleigh founding of SNCC, where he reports Myles Horton's directive that the student activists had to do it themselves. He also documents Septima Poinsette Clark's desegregation of the Sewanee Chapel and the founding of the local NAACP chapter under her supervision with Mrs. Fowler as Black local leader.

The account adds detail to the historical record on three specific fronts: the role of Episcopal seminary desegregation (with Black would-be priests being sent to white seminaries like Seabury-Western and Garrett-Evangelical) as a precursor to broader Episcopal Church desegregation; the FBI surveillance of Highlander Folk School and its visitors (license plates harvested from the parking lot); and the Avon N. Williams Jr. NAACP court-case defense of Sewanee desegregation activists in Franklin County, Tennessee, where Williams traveled weekly for two years.

chapters.title: Chicago Origins and Family Lineage
chapters.summary: Bates introduces his Evanston, Illinois upbringing and his family's Chicago-lawyer lineage tracing back to his great-uncle Leonard Swett, Lincoln's friend. He explains he attended Carleton College for its affordability and absence of fraternities, contrasting it with Northwestern across the street from his home.

chapters.title: WWII French Interpreter in Normandy
chapters.summary: Drafted in 1942, Bates trained in French at the University of Chicago's ASTP program, landed at Utah Beach on D-Day, advanced through Saint-Lo and the Battle of Falaise toward Avranches, and worked as a French interpreter for military government. He recounts the Battle of the Bulge and being held back from it to govern occupied Germany.

chapters.title: Race in Military Government France
chapters.summary: Stationed at Wismes, France supervising legal military-government brothels, Bates describes the segregated arrangement of two supervised white brothels for white troops and unsupervised brothels for Black troops, his MP partnership with a Lincoln University sophomore, and his first close interactions with Black soldiers as fellow English majors discussing literature.

chapters.title: Return to Carleton, PhD, Arrival in Sewanee
chapters.summary: Bates returned on the GI Bill to Carleton College, earned a PhD in French literature at the University of Wisconsin, and accepted a one-year French teaching post at Sewanee, Tennessee in 1954, the year of the Brown decision. He describes the all-white, all-male, all-Episcopal student body and faculty he arrived to.

chapters.title: Episcopal Seminary Desegregation
chapters.summary: Bates describes how the closure of Black Episcopal seminaries in the South forced white seminaries including Seabury-Western and Garrett-Evangelical to accept Black would-be priests, and how the resulting Episcopal Church desegregation drove a Sewanee faculty walkout in 1953. He names V. Gene Robinson, Sewanee class of 1969 and later the first openly gay Episcopal bishop, as a graduate.

chapters.title: Fellowship of Reconciliation and War Resisters League
chapters.summary: Bates recounts joining the Chicago chapter of the Fellowship of Reconciliation for anti-Vietnam War work under John Swomley, then moving to the War Resisters League for its non-Christian breadth. He describes Bayard Rustin contacting him about his anti-war poetry collection, leading to thirty years of poems in the War Resisters League calendar and publication of Poems of War Resistance, selling 13,000 copies.

chapters.title: Sewanee Faculty Culture and the Sewanee Review
chapters.summary: Bates describes the Sewanee faculty's English department as hitched to the Sewanee Review, the country's oldest continuous literary magazine, and discusses the unitarian and ex-Episcopal-priest currents within the liberal-arts faculty. He covers the long struggle to admit women and Black students, finally achieved in 1969.
related_events: first Black graduate of the University of the South

chapters.title: Highlander Folk School Origins and Myles Horton
chapters.summary: Bates traces Myles Horton's path from East Tennessee sharecropping background through Union Theological Seminary under Reinhold Niebuhr, study of Danish folk schools, and work with Jane Addams in Chicago, ending in Grundy County, Tennessee where Lillian Johnson donated the founding Monteagle property. He describes Grundy County as one of the poorest counties in the country, with strong Klan presence, moonshine, and Al Capone trucking.

chapters.title: Highlander Workshop Methodology
chapters.summary: Bates describes Horton's facilitation style at Highlander workshops, including the meeting that brought University of Chattanooga and University of Tennessee Knoxville white students together with Tougaloo Black students to plan integration, and Horton's directive at a later workshop that Stokely Carmichael, John Lewis, and other student activists had to do it themselves, a turning point preceding the founding of SNCC at Shaw University.
related_events: SNCC founding at Shaw University Raleigh 1960

chapters.title: Sewanee NAACP and Septima Clark
chapters.summary: Bates recounts founding the Sewanee NAACP chapter under Septima Poinsette Clark's supervision when she was Highlander's education director, and describes Clark's desegregation of the Sewanee Chapel by attending a service with another Black woman, causing half the white congregation to leave permanently. He names Mrs. Fowler, a Black woman from Sewanee, as the local NAACP chapter leader beginning 1967.

chapters.title: Avon Williams and Franklin County Trials
chapters.summary: Bates describes Avon N. Williams Jr. defending Sewanee desegregation activists in Franklin County court every week for two years, in a To Kill a Mockingbird-style atmosphere where audience members were armed and police prevented a massacre. He covers FBI surveillance of Highlander, John Birch Society organizing on the Sewanee campus, and the sit-in protests at Tubbies that drew FBI investigation of the activists rather than the segregated bar.

chapters.title: Highlander Workshop with King and Rosa Parks
chapters.summary: Bates places himself in the photograph of the 1957 Highlander workshop with Martin Luther King Jr. and Rosa Parks, the image used by segregationists on Communist Training School billboards across the South. He describes Highlander's loss of its Monteagle property after state seizure, the burning of Horton's stone house by Presbyterians from a neighboring parish, and the surviving cemetery containing the Horton family and May Justice.

chapters.title: Highlander in National Memory and French Pedagogy
chapters.summary: Bates reflects on Highlander's long erasure from American history until the Obama era, the institutional opposition he faced from Sewanee's Red Dean for two decades, and his use of French literature pedagogy (Victor Hugo's anti-gun-control poem, Marquis de Sade's banned French texts, Restif de la Bretonne's Anti-Justine) to introduce Sewanee students to French radicalism.

chapters.title: Esau Jenkins, Guy Carawan, and Sea Islands Work
chapters.summary: Bates recounts traveling to Knoxville for meetings with Esau Jenkins and Andrew Young at a black Presbyterian church, taking John's Island Black children on a Charleston bus tour where white kids harassed them, and Septima Clark teaching the children to ignore the harassment. He describes Guy Carawan's residency in Charleston and the planning meeting at the Myrtle Beach Black section ringed by police cars for an entire day.

chapters.title: Highlander Board Service Across Decades
chapters.summary: Bates describes his board service since 1958, including the relocation that made meetings a six-hour drive, his presidency through the 1960s, and his reform of board procedures away from typed reports back to oral discussion. He notes Southern Echo's leader as a fellow board emeritus and the continuing fusion of Black civil-rights work with gay-rights action at Highlander as the same freedom movement.
